steps.title: Identify what directly supports the conclusion
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues concluded that M. barbatus associates T. caesius alarm calls with danger. The experiment only showed a reaction; to support the conclusion, we need evidence that the reaction is specific to alarm calls — not triggered by any sound whatsoever.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Other bird species also froze or scattered when alarm calls were played': broadens the finding to other species but says nothing about whether M. barbatus specifically links alarm calls to danger vs. any loud noise. ✗
  • Choice B — 'No significant variation in M. barbatus response across different T. caesius individuals': shows consistency of the response but not specificity — M. barbatus might still react the same way to random noise. ✗
  • Choice C — 'M. barbatus froze before sounds were played, just from researchers approaching': suggests the birds react to human approach alone — this undermines the conclusion by introducing a confounding trigger. ✗
  • Choice D — 'M. barbatus showed no reaction to control sounds of random noise': proves the reaction is specific to alarm calls, not to sound in general — directly supporting the conclusion that the birds associate alarm calls with danger. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
